metrics.metric: Days since last confirmed cyber incident (Gulf FS)

items.text: Warning sirens were activated in Kuwait multiple times during the coverage period as air defense systems engaged incoming missile and drone threats. The General Staff confirmed that explosions heard across the country resulted from successful interception operations and urged residents to follow civil defense instructions.

The Kuwait Civil Defense mobile alert system continued issuing tiered notifications matching alert severity to threat level. The Ministry of Interior reiterated that all explosions heard are the result of air defense interceptions and called on residents to remain calm.
Cumulative siren activations since conflict began: approximately 144 (estimated based on 131 cumulative as of 25 March plus continued activations on 25–26 March).
sources: Kuwait Ministry of Defense via Alwast News

items.bold_heading: Kuwait — early-morning missile and drone attacks (26 March)
items.text: Kuwait's air defense systems intercepted hostile missile and drone attacks in the early morning hours of 26 March. The Kuwait National Guard announced it successfully downed two UAVs within its areas of responsibility as part of ongoing efforts to protect strategic sites. No casualties reported from these interceptions. Status: confirmed.

items.bold_heading: Kuwait International Airport fuel tank strike (25 March)
items.text: Iranian drones struck a fuel tank at Kuwait International Airport, sparking a fire. Emergency teams deployed to extinguish the blaze. Material damage confirmed; no casualties reported. Status: confirmed.

Kuwait's air defense systems intercepted 17 ballistic missiles and 13 hostile drones during the 24-hour period ending 25 March. Explosive disposal teams responded to 13 new reports of falling debris, bringing the cumulative total to 511 debris reports since the conflict began. Status: confirmed.
sources: Kuwait Ministry of Defense via Alwast News

items.bold_heading: UAE — ballistic missile debris casualties (26 March)
items.text: UAE air defenses intercepted a ballistic missile over Abu Dhabi; falling debris killed two people and injured three, raising the UAE's national death toll to 11. The UAE Ministry of Defence reported that Iran has fired more than 2,100 missiles and drones at the country since the war began. Status: confirmed.

items.bold_heading: Iran launches new wave after UN condemnation (26 March)
items.text: Iran launched a new wave of attacks on Gulf states a day after the UN Human Rights Council adopted a resolution condemning its actions and calling for an immediate halt. Gulf nations continued fending off attacks on the morning of 26 March. Status: confirmed.

items.bold_heading: Israel kills Iranian commander linked to Hormuz blockade (26 March)
items.text: Israel announced it killed a key Iranian military commander involved in the Strait of Hormuz blockade operation. Status: confirmed. [SINGLE SOURCE — awaiting corroboration]

The Ministry of Interior continues to advise residents to maintain emergency supplies of food, water, and medications and to follow siren protocols. The U.S. Embassy in Kuwait maintains a Level 4 "Do Not Travel" advisory and remains closed since 20 March.

Seven overhead power transmission lines were damaged by interception debris on 24 March. Four of seven lines have been restored; repair crews are working around the clock on the remaining three. The Ministry of Electricity, Water and Renewable Energy activated its Emergency Alternative Recovery Plans. Desalination plants and power generation remain operational with 24/7 monitoring.

Kuwait International Airport (KWI) remains closed to all commercial passenger traffic with no reopening date announced. Terminal 1, portions of the runway, fuel depots, and the radar system remain damaged. A new drone strike hit a fuel tank on 25 March, adding to the damage.

Kuwait Airways, Jazeera Airways, Gulf Air, Qatar Airways, Emirates, Turkish Airlines, and other carriers have suspended or cancelled flights to/from Kuwait through late March or April. Limited evacuation flights operate from King Fahd International Airport (Dammam) since 26 March, with passengers processed at Al Khiran Mall and bussed to Saudi Arabia.

The Nuwaiseeb (Kuwait–Saudi Arabia) land border remains open with extended wait times, enabling overland transit to Saudi airports. The Abdali (Iraq) border is reported operational but conditions remain unclear due to military activity in the region. [SINGLE SOURCE on Iraq border]

No specific reports of acute grocery shortages or panic buying identified in the past 24 hours. Cumulative supply pressures remain elevated due to airport closure, Strait of Hormuz disruption, and Kuwait Petroleum Corporation's force majeure on oil exports. [SOURCE GAP — no KUNA or Kuwait Times updates on grocery supply in the past 24 hours]
No new closure announcements identified. Standing conflict-period restrictions remain in effect. [SOURCE GAP]

Iran rejected the U.S. 15-point ceasefire plan on 25 March, calling it "extremely maximalist and unreasonable," and Iran's Foreign Minister stated Iran has not engaged in direct talks and does not plan to negotiate; however, Pakistan is working to arrange a meeting between U.S. and Iranian envoys in Islamabad, possibly as soon as this weekend.

Oil prices surged on 26 March — Brent crude rose 3.8% to $106.12/bbl and WTI climbed 3.6% to $93.61/bbl — after Iran signalled it has no intention of holding direct talks with the U.S., reversing the prior day's decline; energy executives and Germany's economic minister warned that fuel shortages could hit Europe starting in April if the conflict persists.

Israel announced the killing of a key Iranian military commander involved in the Strait of Hormuz blockade; Hormuz transit remains collapsed at approximately 5 vessels/day versus a pre-war average of ~120, with the U.S. military campaign to reopen the strait ongoing since 19 March.

Kuwait International Airport closure (new fuel tank damage on 25 March), partial power outages, and daily missile/drone threat sustain elevated operational continuity risk. Staff relocation and remote working arrangements should remain in effect; overland evacuation via Saudi Arabia is the only viable exit route.

Oil price volatility intensified — Brent swung from ~$126/bbl peak to $102/bbl and back to $106/bbl within days. The near-total closure of the Strait of Hormuz (95% transit decline) continues to pressure energy markets, Gulf revenue streams, and global trade. Goldman Sachs forecasts Kuwait GDP could contract 14% in 2026.

Physical damage to Kuwait's power and telecommunications infrastructure elevates the risk of intermittent disruptions to electronic payment processing and cross-border settlement. No specific SWIFT disruption reported, but contingency plans should be validated.
sources: Assessed based on infrastructure damage reporting

items.text: Daily missile and drone attacks on Kuwait, combined with airport closure, limit evacuation options for expatriate staff. The U.S. Embassy maintains a Level 4 advisory and is itself closed. UAE debris casualties (2 killed, 3 injured on 26 March) underscore the ongoing physical risk to personnel across the Gulf.

Iran-aligned threat actors including IRGC-linked Hydro Kitten have signalled intent to target the financial sector. CrowdStrike warns of potential escalation from nuisance-level hacktivism to coordinated destructive operations. The Void Manticore/Handala wiper attack vector (via Microsoft Intune) remains a live threat to endpoint management systems.

articles.title: Assessing the Air Campaign After Three Weeks: Iran War By the Numbers
articles.author: Mark F. Cancian, Chris H. Park
articles.summary: Provides a data-driven assessment of the U.S.-Israeli air campaign against Iran after three weeks, analyzing strike volumes, munitions expenditure, and operational tempo. The analysis highlights the escalating costs and the mismatch between military objectives and diplomatic progress.
articles.relevance: Quantifies the scale and trajectory of the conflict, directly informing planning assumptions for how long Gulf disruption may persist.

articles.title: Pakistan Could Be a U.S.-Iran Peace Broker
articles.author: Not confirmed
articles.summary: Examines Pakistan's unique position as a potential mediator in the Iran conflict, given its geographic proximity, diplomatic relationships with both sides, and willingness to host talks. Argues that Pakistan's mediation effort is the most credible diplomatic channel currently active.
articles.relevance: If Pakistan-mediated talks gain traction this weekend, markets and Hormuz transit could shift rapidly — material for scenario planning.

items.bold_heading: CISA KEV Update — CVE-2026-33017 (Langflow Code Injection)
items.text: CISA added CVE-2026-33017 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog on 25 March 2026. This is a code injection vulnerability in Langflow (an AI/ML workflow platform) that is under active exploitation. Affected sector: cross-sector (any organization using Langflow).
items.recommended_action: Verify whether Langflow is deployed in any environment and patch or isolate immediately per CISA BOD 22-01 timelines.

items.bold_heading: Unit 42 — Wiper attack risk via endpoint management systems
items.text: Palo Alto Networks' Unit 42 warns of increased wiper attack risk tied to the Iran conflict. The Void Manticore/Handala threat actor compromised highly privileged identities and pushed legitimate remote-wipe commands to over 200,000 devices globally via Microsoft Intune. Affected sector: cross-sector, including financial services.
items.recommended_action: Review Microsoft Intune and all MDM platform access controls, enforce MFA on all administrative accounts, and audit recent enrollment and policy deployment activity.

items.bold_heading: DDoS campaigns against Kuwait government domains (ongoing)
items.text: Multiple Iran-aligned hacktivist groups — including Hider_Nex (allied with NoName057(16)), Keymous Plus, and Team 313 — have conducted DDoS campaigns against Kuwaiti government domains targeting defense, health, finance, and civil infrastructure portals. Team 313 claimed an 18-hour disruption of 26 Kuwaiti government domains.
items.recommended_action: Verify DDoS mitigation is active on all public-facing services; monitor for credential harvesting campaigns that often follow DDoS activity.

items.bold_heading: CrowdStrike — Hydro Kitten financial sector targeting signal
items.text: CrowdStrike reports that Hydro Kitten, an IRGC-linked APT group, has signalled plans to target the financial sector. While large-scale state-sponsored campaigns have not yet materialized, a surge in Iran-aligned hacktivist activity (60+ active groups) includes DDoS operations, defacements, and alleged disruptions across the Middle East and the U.S. CrowdStrike advises that critical infrastructure and financial sector firms should prepare for escalation from nuisance-level disruption into coordinated or destructive operations.

items.bold_heading: IISS — Asymmetry in cyberspace favours U.S./Israel
items.text: The International Institute for Strategic Studies published analysis noting that Iran's internet connectivity has been at 1–4% since February 28, severely degrading Iranian state-sponsored actors' ability to coordinate sophisticated cyber operations. However, IISS warns that as Iran's connectivity is gradually restored, the operational tempo of state-sponsored cyber campaigns is likely to increase.

items.bold_heading: Recorded Future — Electronic Operations Room coordination
items.text: Recorded Future's Insikt Group reports that a newly established "Electronic Operations Room" (formed 28 February 2026) is coordinating hacktivist and state-aligned cyber operations. While Iran's internet blackout has constrained advanced operations, Recorded Future assesses that restoration of connectivity will likely be followed by a surge in coordinated campaigns. Financial institutions remain high-probability spillover targets due to U.S.-linked assets and high-visibility public-facing services.
Pakistan-hosted U.S.-Iran talks this weekend
items.why_it_matters: If talks materialize, this could shift the conflict trajectory and trigger rapid oil price movement.

items.text: Impact of killed Iranian Hormuz commander
items.why_it_matters: Israel's announced killing of a key Iranian commander involved in the Strait of Hormuz blockade could either degrade Iran's blockade capability or provoke intensified retaliatory strikes on Gulf targets.

items.text: Kuwait power grid restoration progress
items.why_it_matters: Three of seven damaged transmission lines remain unrepaired; failure to restore could compound operational disruptions for financial institutions relying on grid power.

source_confidence.note: No fresh KUNA updates found on grocery supply or school closures for the past 24 hours. Iraq border status based on limited sourcing.
Cyber intelligence items are based on vendor blogs and CISA advisories; no Gulf-specific CERT updates found for the past 24 hours.
